data.definition: Software as a Service - online platform hosted by a server for users to access. (Microsoft office, Netflix)
data.example: Instead of buying and installing software, you log into Netflix or Gmail in your browser and pay a subscription — the company runs it for you.

data.definition: Large Language Model — an AI trained on huge amounts of text that predicts the next word to generate human-like responses.
data.example: When you chat with Claude or ChatGPT, an LLM is what reads your message and writes the reply.

data.definition: Wipes conversation history, keeps file edits. Use when context is getting large.
data.example: You've finished a feature and the chat is huge — type /clear to start fresh while keeping all your saved files.

data.definition: Smarter version of /clear; compresses history instead of deleting it, preserves key info. Better default than /clear for mid-session use.
data.example: Mid-build and context is filling up — /compact frees space but still remembers what you're working on.

data.definition: Can restore context even after a /clear if you regret it.
data.example: You ran /clear then realized you needed that earlier discussion — /rewind brings it back.

data.definition: Claude thinks through the approach before touching any files. Critical for large tasks to avoid compounding mistakes.
data.example: Before a big refactor: /plan refactor the login system — Claude maps out the steps before changing anything.

data.definition: Shows exactly what changed before you commit, so nothing surprises you.
data.example: Right before committing: /diff to eyeball every change Claude made.

data.definition: Sets a target Claude works toward across many turns. Good for "finish the backend" type sessions.
data.example: /goal finish and test the payment backend — keeps Claude pointed at that across many messages.

data.definition: Ask a side question without polluting the main task context. Useful when you're curious about something mid-build.
data.example: /btw what does CORS mean? — get a quick answer without derailing the task you're in.

data.definition: Picks up a previous session.
data.example: Open your laptop the next day and /resume to continue right where yesterday's session left off.

data.definition: Forks the conversation into a separate session for experimenting without messing up your main thread.
data.example: Want to try a risky approach — /branch to experiment in a copy without touching your main thread.

data.definition: Shows how many tokens you've used and what it's costing.
data.example: Wondering if you're spending too much — /cost shows tokens used and the running bill.

data.definition: Switch models mid-session (e.g. drop to Sonnet for boilerplate, use Opus for hard problems).
data.example: Writing boring boilerplate — /model to switch to Sonnet, then back to Opus for the hard part.

data.definition: Adjusts reasoning depth. Lower effort = faster/cheaper.
data.example: Simple task — lower /effort for a faster, cheaper reply; raise it for a tricky bug.

data.definition: Toggles Fast mode — same Opus model, faster output. Handy when you want quick responses.
data.example: Want snappier replies without dropping to a weaker model — /fast toggles faster Opus output.

data.definition: Checks for config issues.
data.example: Something feels off with your setup — /doctor scans for configuration problems.

data.definition: Lists everything available in your current version.
data.example: Forgot which commands exist — /help lists everything your version supports.

data.definition: Run once in a new project; generates a CLAUDE.md file that keeps Claude oriented every time you start a new session.
data.example: Starting a brand-new project — run /init once to create the CLAUDE.md that orients Claude each session.

data.definition: Opens settings to change theme, default model, and other Claude Code options.
data.example: Want dark mode or a different default model — /config opens the settings.

data.definition: Edit your CLAUDE.md memory files directly, so you can tweak the instructions Claude reads each session.
data.example: Claude keeps forgetting a preference — /memory to edit the instructions it reads every time.

data.definition: Create and manage custom subagents — specialized helpers Claude can hand specific tasks to.
data.example: Want a dedicated code-reviewer helper — /agents to create and manage one.

data.definition: Reviews a GitHub pull request and reports issues it finds.
data.example: Opened a PR on GitHub — /review <PR-url> to get Claude's feedback before merging.

data.definition: Runs a security review of the pending changes on your current branch.
data.example: Before merging sensitive changes — /security-review scans your diff for security issues.
